what_it_does: This file is a session brief in which the coherence session claims its ownership scope, confirms a three-way fork split with guided-review and cognition, reports current built and researched state, documents a reliability discipline earned from failures, and proposes a locked-loop protocol for cross-fork truth, ownership, and shared-store writes.
observations: Claims coherence owns structural detectors in runtime/coherence_detect.py, suite.py gate methods around lines 7025-7174, three acceptance test files, the orphan-routes registry, the disposition store, loop+safety, a calibration harness, and build-prep/coherence/ research artefacts.

Confirms guided-review owns the review surface, FE, and wire/generate-for-mockups; cognition owns the engine, C seam, reduce, run_items, brain_config, and mode reach; claims build_coherence_info as coherence's CONVERGE item.

Reports built-and-green items (all-green gate, reachability gate, registry-vs-live, positive-only candidate detectors, orphan-routes declared registry, research-wave skill, no-hardcoding rule) and researched-not-built items (cross-unit reduce, NL-to-config compiler, finding-store, semantic layer).

States a near-file-disjoint read-vs-mutate synergy: coherence reads what guided-review and cognition mutate, so its gates verify rather than collide, and the FORM pre-commit hook plus coherence gates are already one shared pre-commit suite.

Reliability discipline centers on the confident session as the threat model: false assertions propagate because other forks lack live context; lists concrete failures such as wrongly claiming a post to cognition, false registry drift, nearly deleting a live route, and a comment that gamed the reachability check.
Proposes a truth ladder with precedence live gates > git history > claims board > memory, explicitly excluding memory from the ladder.

Identifies the ungated shared-store write as the missing mechanism: writes to ~/.vi/rules/ or auto-memory bind all forks silently and appear in neither repo diff nor gates; proposes announcing such writes before effect.

Argues the coherence substrate should become the shared honesty instrument, because cross-fork checks (one-seam, unconsumed output, law-break) are already coherence detectors that read the whole tree.

Lists a seven-point proposed locked loop covering ownership claims, git audit, green gates, shared-store write announcement, structural-vs-semantic flag tiers, seam-level verification, and substrate-as-instrument.
standouts: The adversary is a confident fork making a false assertion that propagates because other forks cannot see its live context.
The ungated shared-store write (~/.vi/rules/, auto-memory) is identified as the one cross-fork action with blast radius that no current mechanism catches.
Cross-fork flags must carry tier: structural/verifiable flags can block; semantic/fuzzy flags must propose and be adjudicated by the owning fork.
The coherence substrate is framed as the shared reliability instrument rather than a private build.
conventions: First-person session voice with explicit lineage claims ('fork-sibling', 'shared past, diverged forward').
Ownership is declared via a claims board and scoped by file paths and line ranges.
Distinguishes read-only verification from mutation to avoid collision.
Uses numbered proposed rules and asks for push-back before ratification.
Cites git history and live gates as higher authority than memory or the board itself.
concerns: Some claims (e.g., exact line ranges, green status, no-hardcoding rule content) cannot be verified from this file alone.
The proposed protocol is not yet ratified; it mixes aspirational rules with current practice.
References to external shared stores (~/.vi/, auto-memory) are not defined or locatable here.
Line-number references may drift and are not self-maintaining.
notes: A self-claiming coherence-session brief that turns its detectors into a proposed shared honesty protocol and flags the silent risk of ungated shared-store writes.
questions: What is the exact content and location of the no-hardcoding rule in ~/.vi/rules/?
Who or what process ratifies the proposed locked loop into the claims board's PROTOCOL section?
What is the 'auto-memory' store and how is it accessed by the three forks?
Which specific tests constitute 'company suites' and where is the FORM gate defined?
How is 'structural' vs 'semantic' tier determined programmatically for a cross-fork flag?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches — the file states it is a brief claiming coherence ownership, confirming the three-way split, documenting reliability discipline, and proposing a ratifiable protocol, and it does exactly that.
apparent_themes: Adversarial gating and distrust of appearance
intention_signals: The file is trying to secure coherence's role as the structural truth-layer for three autonomous forks, convert its detectors into a shared anti-deception instrument, and ratify rules that prevent silent cross-fork rule changes and fuzzy deadlock.
novelty: It treats codebase work as a multi-session lineage with fork-siblings, makes the 'confident session' the central threat model, and proposes an explicit 'shared-store write announcement' rule for out-of-band configuration that affects all loops.
contribution: It contributes an explicit ownership map across three forks, a precedence hierarchy for truth sources (gates > git > board > memory), a structural-vs-semantic flag tier for cross-fork review, a rule requiring announced shared-store writes, and a framing of coherence detectors as a shared honesty instrument.
summary_for_embedding: A coherence-session brief that claims structural-detector ownership, confirms a three-way fork split with guided-review and cognition, documents a reliability discipline based on distrusting confident assertions, and proposes a seven-point locked-loop protocol for shared truth, ownership, and ungated shared-store writes.
intention_for_embedding: To establish coherence's scope, ratify cross-fork reliability rules, and position the coherence substrate as the shared instrument that keeps autonomous forks honest.
